input.prompt:
Help me migrate my .NET 10 console app to .NET 11. The app:
- Creates GZipStream to compress data, then checks if the output is empty (zero bytes) to detect "no data" scenarios
- Reads TAR archives that were hand-crafted by a legacy tool (some may have invalid checksums)
- Uses ZipArchive.CreateAsync for large ZIP files and relies on lazy loading behavior
- Uses MemoryStream with very large allocations close to the maximum capacity
What breaks when migrating to .NET 11?

Use this reference section to find breaking changes that might apply to you if you're upgrading your app to a newer version of .NET.

If you're looking for breaking changes from .NET Framework to .NET, see [Breaking changes for migration from .NET Framework to .NET (Core)](fx-core).

## GitHub issues and announcements

You can also view individual issues that detail the breaking changes introduced in .NET in the following GitHub repositories:

*   For .NET Core and .NET 5+, the [dotnet/docs](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues?q=is%3Aissue+label%3Abreaking-change) repository.
*   For ASP.NET Core, the [aspnet/Announcements](https://github.com/aspnet/Announcements/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22Breaking+change%22+label%3A3.0.0) repository.
*   For Entity Framework Core, the [dotnet/efcore](https://github.com/dotnet/efcore/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3Abreaking-change) repository.

## See also

*   [API removal in .NET](api-removal)
*   [.NET runtime compatibility](../versions/#net-runtime-compatibility)
*   [Migrate from .NET Framework to .NET Core](../porting/)
*   [Breaking changes for migration from .NET Framework to .NET Core](fx-core)

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49393:
### Description

Deflate and GZip compression formats include delimiters for start and end of compressed data. The right way to compress empty payload with these formats includes only those delimiters.

### Version

.NET 11 Preview 1

### Previous behavior

`DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` would not produce any output if no data was written.

### New behavior

`DeflateStream` and `GZipStream` always write headers and footers to the output.

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Some tools do not correctly react to empty content and always expect the compressed content to contain at least the appropriate header and footer.

### Recommended action

If previous behavior is desired, we recommend special casing empty content to not try to compress it via `DeflateStream` or `GZipStream`

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

### Affected APIs

`DeflateStream`  
`GZipStream`

* * *

data.content:
I found all four breaking changes. Here's what breaks in your app on .NET 11:

---

### 1. GZipStream — empty output no longer means "no data"
Previously, compressing zero bytes produced zero output. Now GZipStream **always writes headers/footers** even for empty payloads. Your `output.Length == 0` check will never be true. **Fix:** check the *input* length, or compare against the new baseline empty-archive size.

### 2. TarReader — invalid checksums now throw
TarReader now **validates checksums** and throws `InvalidDataException` on mismatch. Your legacy hand-crafted archives with bad checksums will fail at `GetNextEntry()`. **Fix:** wrap reads in a `try/catch (InvalidDataException)`, or fix the legacy archives' checksums.

### 3. ZipArchive.CreateAsync — eager entry loading
`CreateAsync` now **reads all entries eagerly** (asynchronously) instead of lazily on `.Entries` access. Malformed entries throw during `CreateAsync` instead of later. **Fix:** move your error handling from `.Entries` access to the `CreateAsync` call.

### 4. MemoryStream — lower max capacity, different exception
Max capacity dropped from `int.MaxValue` (`0x7FFFFFFF`) to `0x7FFFFFC7` (Array.MaxLength). Exceeding it now throws **`ArgumentOutOfRangeException`** instead of `OutOfMemoryException`. **Fix:** cap allocations at `Array.MaxLength` and catch `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` alongside `OutOfMemoryException`.

All four are .NET 11 Preview 1 behavioral changes ([dotnet/docs #49393](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49393), [#49394](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49394), [#50254](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/50254), [#49545](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49545)).
